case_studies.title: Feeding Challenges in an Infant with Cleft Palate
case_studies.vignette: A 3-month-old male infant is brought to a rural paediatric outpatient clinic by his mother for poor weight gain. The child was born with an isolated cleft palate and has not yet undergone surgical repair. The mother reports that feeding takes over 45 minutes per session, during which the baby frequently coughs, chokes, and has milk coming out of his nose. She also mentions that the baby appears fatigued after feeds and often falls asleep before finishing. The family uses a standard bottle for feeding, as they were unaware of specialized feeding devices. On examination, the infant’s weight is below the 3rd percentile for age, and he appears thin but alert. A feeding observation reveals inefficient sucking, nasal regurgitation, and frequent pauses due to coughing. The mother sits the baby in a semi-reclined position during feeds. No signs of acute respiratory distress or cyanosis are noted. The mother expresses concern about the baby’s upcoming cleft repair surgery, as she has been told the baby must gain weight before the procedure.
case_studies.learning_objective: Evaluate feeding efficiency, safety, and caregiver technique in an infant with cleft palate to assess growth adequacy and surgical readiness.

case_studies.title: Feeding Challenges in a 3-Month-Old with Cleft Palate
case_studies.vignette: A 3-month-old male infant is brought to a rural paediatric clinic in India for poor weight gain. The mother reports that feeding takes over an hour per session, with frequent pauses due to choking and coughing. She also mentions milk often comes out through the nose during feeds. The infant appears fatigued after feeding and cries frequently. The family uses a regular bottle for feeding, as they are unaware of specialty feeding devices. On examination, the infant is alert but underweight, with a weight-for-age below the 3rd percentile. The cleft palate is noted on oral examination. During a supervised feeding session, the infant struggles to coordinate sucking and breathing, with visible nasal regurgitation and intermittent coughing. The mother appears anxious and unsure about feeding techniques. No signs of respiratory distress or acute infection are observed. The family expresses concern about the upcoming cleft surgery, as they were told the baby must gain weight beforehand.
case_studies.learning_objective: Evaluate feeding efficiency and safety in infants with cleft palate, focusing on caregiver technique, feeding duration, and growth monitoring to assess surgical readiness.

case_studies.title: Dietary Assessment and Feeding Skills Evaluation in an Infant with Cleft Palate
case_studies.vignette: A 3-month-old male infant is brought to a rural health center in India by his mother with concerns of poor weight gain and frequent feeding difficulties. The mother reports that feeding takes over an hour per session, with the infant frequently coughing, choking, and showing nasal regurgitation during bottle feeds. She also mentions that the infant appears fatigued after feeding and often falls asleep before finishing. The family uses a regular bottle with a cross-cut nipple, as they are unaware of specialized feeding devices. On examination, the infant is alert but appears underweight, with a weight-for-age below the 3rd percentile. Feeding observation reveals poor suction, frequent pauses, and nasal regurgitation. The infant is positioned semi-reclined during feeds, and the caregiver demonstrates no pacing or burping techniques. No signs of acute respiratory distress are noted, but the infant's feeding efficiency is visibly poor. The mother expresses concern about whether the baby will be ready for surgery as advised by the cleft team.
case_studies.learning_objective: Evaluate feeding efficiency, safety, and caregiver technique in infants with cleft palate, focusing on growth monitoring and pre-surgical nutritional readiness.

case_studies.title: Feeding Challenges in a 3-Month-Old with Cleft Palate
case_studies.vignette: A 3-month-old male infant is brought to the paediatric outpatient clinic by his mother, who reports difficulty feeding since birth. The infant was diagnosed with an isolated cleft palate at birth. The mother describes prolonged feeding times of over 40 minutes per session, frequent nasal regurgitation of milk, and occasional coughing during feeds. She notes that the baby tires easily and sometimes falls asleep before finishing a feed. The child is exclusively formula-fed using a standard bottle. On examination, the infant appears alert but has poor weight gain, with his weight at the 3rd percentile for age. A focused feeding observation reveals inefficient sucking with milk escaping through the nose and intermittent choking. The mother holds the baby in a semi-reclined position during feeding and is unaware of alternative feeding devices or techniques. There is no history of fever, vomiting, or diarrhoea. The mother expresses concern about the baby's upcoming cleft repair surgery and asks if he is ready for it.
case_studies.learning_objective: Evaluate feeding efficiency and safety in infants with cleft palate, identifying barriers to adequate intake and assessing readiness for surgical intervention.

subtopics.subtopic_title: COMMON PITFALLS AND QUALITY ASSURANCE STRATEGIES
subtopics.concept: Errors in dietary assessment and feeding skills evaluation are often predictable and preventable, requiring careful attention to common pitfalls and adherence to quality assurance strategies. In dietary assessment, frequent errors include failing to account for snacks and liquids, underestimating the intake of packaged high-fat, salt, and sugar (HFSS) foods, overlooking the dilution of milk or porridge, and recording what was offered rather than what was actually consumed. Social desirability bias is particularly common when discussing obesity or undernutrition; normalizing the conversation, such as by stating “many children eat biscuits daily,” can encourage more accurate disclosure. A single 24-hour dietary recall may misclassify usual intake, so repeating the recall on a non-consecutive day is recommended when decisions rely on precise quantification. Errors in nutrient computation often stem from improper mapping of mixed dishes, reliance on non-Indian composition data for Indian recipes, and neglecting to account for cooking oil or sugar. For formal analysis, it is essential to document the food database used (e.g., IFCT) and explicitly record recipes. In evaluating feeding skills, common pitfalls include focusing solely on food groups while neglecting signs of unsafe swallowing, fatigue, or inappropriate food textures. Counseling exclusively on “what to feed” without addressing “how to feed” can perpetuate feeding conflicts and poor intake. Observations conducted in rushed clinical settings may be misleading if the child is not hungry; scheduling observations around usual mealtimes or using home videos can provide more accurate insights. Quality assurance in dietary and feeding evaluations is process-driven and requires standardized scripts, household measure guides, and observation checklists. Staff must be trained, and periodic calibration of portion estimation using real utensils is essential. Reconciliation of dietary reports with anthropometric data and clinical signs is critical, alongside documentation of decision pathways and follow-up intervals. In multilingual settings, maintaining translated food lists and standardized probes minimizes interviewer drift across visits. These strategies collectively enhance the reliability and accuracy of dietary and feeding assessments in paediatric practice.

case_studies.title: Dietary Assessment Pitfalls in a Toddler with Poor Weight Gain
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the outpatient clinic in a semi-urban area of India with concerns about poor weight gain over the past 6 months. The mother reports that he eats three meals daily, including rice, lentils, and vegetables, but she is unsure about exact quantities. She mentions he often has biscuits and fruit juice as snacks. Upon further questioning, she reveals that the family dilutes milk with water to make it last longer. The boy has no fever or recent illnesses, but his energy levels seem lower than his peers, and he appears fatigued during play. On examination, his weight is below the 3rd percentile, and he has mild pallor. A dietary recall is conducted, but the mother struggles to provide detailed information about cooking methods and portion sizes. No direct feeding observation was performed during the visit, and no home video was available. A rushed evaluation of feeding skills reveals that the child occasionally coughs while drinking liquids, but this detail is not explored further.
case_studies.learning_objective: Identify common pitfalls in dietary recall and feeding skills evaluation, including incomplete dietary information, neglect of unsafe swallowing signs, and rushed clinical observations, and apply quality assurance strategies to improve accuracy.

case_studies.title: Dietary Assessment and Feeding Skills Evaluation: Common Pitfalls
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old girl is brought to a community health clinic in rural India with concerns of poor weight gain over the past six months. Her mother reports that she eats rice, lentils, and vegetables daily but avoids milk and fruits. When asked about snacks, the mother initially denies offering packaged foods but later mentions biscuits and chips as occasional treats. She also notes that the child drinks diluted milk twice a day. The family uses a shared plate for meals, making portion estimation challenging. On examination, the child appears alert but fatigued after brief play, with mild pallor and weight at the 5th percentile for age. A brief feeding observation reveals the child struggling with coarse-textured food, spitting out bites, and showing signs of fatigue. No choking episodes are reported. A single 24-hour dietary recall was conducted during the visit, but no follow-up recall was planned. Objective data include weight-for-age z-score: -1.8 and hemoglobin: 9.2 g/dL.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize common pitfalls in dietary assessment and feeding skills evaluation, including incomplete dietary recall, social desirability bias, neglect of feeding mechanics, and strategies for quality assurance such as repeated recalls and structured observ

case_studies.title: Dietary Assessment and Feeding Skills Evaluation in a Preschool Child
case_studies.vignette: A 3-year-old girl is brought to a rural clinic in India by her mother with concerns of poor weight gain over the past 6 months. The mother reports that the child eats 'a good amount' of food but becomes tired during meals and often refuses solids. She adds that the child prefers milk and biscuits and occasionally eats rice and dal. On further questioning, the mother admits to diluting milk with water to make it last longer. A 24-hour dietary recall reveals frequent consumption of packaged snacks and minimal intake of fresh fruits or vegetables. The child is active but appears fatigued during the clinical exam. Her weight is below the 5th percentile for age, and her height is at the 25th percentile. Observation of feeding skills in the clinic shows the child struggling to chew solid textures, with occasional coughing during swallowing. No home video was provided, and the feeding observation was conducted outside usual mealtime.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize common pitfalls in dietary assessment and feeding skills evaluation, such as reliance on single-day dietary recall, social desirability bias, and neglect of unsafe swallowing signs or inappropriate food textures.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CLINICAL VIGNETTE FALTERING GROWTH FROM DILUTED COMPLEMENTARY FEEDS
subtopics.concept: A 9-month-old infant presenting with two months of faltering weight gain highlights the importance of assessing dietary intake and feeding skills when diluted complementary feeds are the primary concern. The caregiver reports the infant’s diet consists mainly of thin rice gruel and “dal water,” with persistent smooth purees and no exposure to finger foods. A structured 24-hour dietary recall, combined with a brief feeding observation, is essential to evaluate energy density, protein intake, and texture handling. The recall reveals low energy density and infrequent protein consumption, with total intake falling below estimated age-appropriate nutritional needs. Feeding observation confirms the complementary feeds are excessively diluted, with a runny consistency that drips off the spoon, and the infant exhibits minimal active mouth movements. When offered mashed banana, the infant gags, suggesting limited exposure to textured foods rather than an underlying systemic illness. Importantly, there are no clinical signs of malabsorption or chronic infection. Management should focus on optimizing the energy density and nutritional adequacy of the same foods. Caregivers should be counseled to prepare thicker versions of khichdi or dal-rice, avoid excessive water during preparation, and incorporate small amounts of oil or ghee to increase caloric intake. An age-appropriate feeding structure should be established, consisting of three meals and two snacks daily, with at least one protein source included, such as pulses in thick paste form, egg (if culturally acceptable), or dairy. Advancing food textures is critical; caregivers should introduce mashed foods and soft lumps while offering safe finger foods to promote self-feeding and chewing skill development. Follow-up metrics should include weight monitoring and a repeat one-day dietary recall within 2–4 weeks, alongside feeding observations to confirm progression in texture handling. If weight velocity does not improve despite documented adherence, referral to a dietitian is warranted. Additionally, if safety concerns such as coughing or a wet voice emerge with thicker textures, evaluation by a speech-language pathologist (SLP) is necessary.

case_studies.title: Faltering Growth in a 9-Month-Old Due to Diluted Complementary Feeds
case_studies.vignette: A 9-month-old male infant is brought to a rural primary health center in India with concerns of poor weight gain over the past two months. The mother reports that the infant has been exclusively breastfed until 6 months of age, after which complementary feeds were introduced. His current diet primarily includes thin rice gruel and 'dal water,' fed 3–4 times a day. The mother also mentions that the feeds are diluted because she believes it is easier for the baby to digest. The infant has not been introduced to finger foods or textured solids yet. On examination, the infant appears thin with visible wasting, but there is no peripheral edema or signs of severe malnutrition. Weight is 6.5 kg (below the 3rd percentile for age). A 24-hour dietary recall reveals low caloric and protein intake, with no inclusion of energy-dense or protein-rich foods. During a feeding observation, the complementary feeds are noted to be runny, and the infant shows minimal chewing or mouthing activity. When offered a small piece of mashed banana, he gags but does not vomit. There are no signs of systemic illness, malabsorption, or chronic infection on clinical evaluation.
case_studies.learning_objective: Recognize the role of dietary assessment and feeding skills evaluation in identifying faltering growth due to diluted complementary feeds and inadequate texture progression.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CLINICAL VIGNETTE TODDLER MILK OVERUSE WITH IRON DEFICIENCY ANEMIA
subtopics.concept: A 2-year-old child presents with pallor and poor appetite, with a history of frequent “milk-and-biscuit” feeding, including a bedtime bottle, and minimal intake of family foods. A targeted dietary assessment, combining a 24-hour dietary recall with an iron-focused frequency screen (including eggs, meat, pulses, green leafy vegetables, and fortified foods), reveals excessive cow’s milk consumption displacing solids and a very low frequency of iron-rich foods. Feeding observations often show grazing behavior, distraction during feeding, and prolonged meal times. The clinical interpretation suggests that inadequate dietary iron intake and displacement by excessive milk consumption are the primary contributors to iron deficiency anemia, with a low likelihood of malabsorption in the absence of chronic diarrhea. Management focuses on structured dietary changes and iron therapy. Milk intake should be limited, and solids should be offered before milk. Transitioning from a bottle to a cup is recommended to reduce night feeding and mitigate dental risks. Daily inclusion of iron-rich foods, such as pulses, eggs, or meat (when culturally acceptable), is advised, with meals paired with vitamin C sources to enhance iron absorption while avoiding tea with meals. Therapeutic iron supplementation should be initiated according to local guidelines, with adherence strategies documented, including timing, monitoring for side effects, and addressing potential constipation. Follow-up is essential and should be objective. Dietary patterns should be reassessed, and hemoglobin levels repeated in 4–6 weeks. If hemoglobin fails to rise as expected despite confirmed adherence to the treatment plan, further evaluation for alternative causes such as blood loss, hemoglobinopathy, or celiac disease should be undertaken. This escalation should be guided by clinical symptoms and examination findings rather than repeating empiric supplementation.

case_studies.title: Toddler with Pallor and Poor Appetite: Dietary Assessment & Feeding Skills Evaluation
case_studies.vignette: A 2-year-old boy is brought to a rural primary health center by his mother with complaints of pallor and reduced appetite for the past 3 months. The mother reports that he drinks about 1.5 liters of cow's milk daily, mostly through a bottle, including at bedtime, and eats minimal family foods. His typical diet consists of milk and biscuits, with occasional rice but no eggs, meat, pulses, or green leafy vegetables. Feeding times are prolonged, often exceeding 45 minutes, and the child appears distracted during meals, demanding videos or toys. On examination, the child is pale with no jaundice, edema, or organomegaly. His weight is at the 10th percentile for age, and his conjunctivae are pale. A dietary recall confirms excessive milk intake and very low consumption of iron-rich foods. Hemoglobin is 8.2 g/dL, and mean corpuscular volume (MCV) is 70 fL.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CLINICAL VIGNETTE PRESCHOOL OBESITY WITH HFSS AND SUGARY BEVERAGES
subtopics.concept: A 5-year-old child presenting with a rising BMI percentile and frequent snacking is noted to consume daily packaged snacks, instant noodles, and sugar-sweetened beverages, with a low intake of fruits and vegetables. In such cases, a rapid diet quality screen assessing the weekly frequency of protective foods and high-fat, salt, and sugar (HFSS) foods is recommended for initial triage. This is followed by a brief 24-hour dietary recall to evaluate the timing, portion sizes, contextual factors, and liquid calorie intake. Common findings include a high concentration of discretionary calories in beverages and between-meal snacks, inconsistent meal patterns, and screen use during meals. The primary interpretation typically attributes the diet pattern and environment as key contributors to obesity, with endocrine causes considered only if poor linear growth is also observed. Management should focus on family-centered, pattern-based counseling. A concrete beverage goal should be established, such as replacing sugary drinks with water and reserving sweetened beverages for rare occasions. A snack substitution plan is essential, encouraging options like fruits, curd, or homemade snacks instead of daily packaged items. Structured meals and planned snacks should be prioritized, with grazing discouraged and screen-free mealtimes promoted to support satiety regulation. School lunch and tiffin choices should also be addressed when relevant. Daily active play should be encouraged, and sedentary screen time reduced, with behavior changes linked to measurable goals rather than calorie counting. Follow-up is advised within 4–8 weeks, including repeat weight and diet frequency assessments. Screening for comorbidities should be based on the severity of obesity, with evaluations for blood pressure, acanthosis nigricans, and sleep-related symptoms. Endocrine testing should be reserved for children with obesity accompanied by poor height velocity or other suggestive clinical features. This structured approach ensures a comprehensive evaluation and management plan tailored to preschool-aged children with obesity associated with HFSS foods and sugary beverages.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CLINICAL VIGNETTE CEREBRAL PALSY DYSPHAGIA SAFETY AND REFERRAL TRIGGERS
subtopics.concept: In the context of cerebral palsy (CP), dysphagia poses significant safety and nutritional challenges, necessitating a structured approach to assessment and referral. A 3-year-old child with CP presenting with poor weight gain, meal durations exceeding 45 minutes, coughing with liquids, and recurrent chest infections highlights the need for a dysphagia red-flag screen. Observing a typical feed using the child’s usual utensils and textures is crucial, as it often reveals poor postural stability, rapid fatigue, and signs of aspiration risk, such as coughing or a wet voice after consuming thin liquids. A 24-hour dietary recall may indicate low total intake due to the child’s inability to sustain feeding long enough to meet caloric needs. These findings suggest that feeding skill and safety limitations, rather than food availability, are the primary barriers to adequate nutrition. Management prioritizes risk reduction while arranging specialist assessments. Upright, supported seating and slower-paced feeding with small boluses are recommended, with feeds discontinued at the first signs of stress cues to avoid forcing volume. Standardized texture definitions, such as those outlined by the International Dysphagia Diet Standardisation Initiative (IDDSI), should be used to ensure clear communication regarding texture modifications. Empiric thickening of liquids should not be prolonged without guidance from a speech-language pathologist (SLP). Referral for instrumental swallow evaluation, such as videofluoroscopic swallow study (VFSS) or fiberoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing (FEES), is essential to identify safe textures and test compensatory strategies. Nutritional support must be realistic and individualized. If the child cannot safely meet caloric needs orally, a dietitian should establish calorie targets, and oral nutritional supplements (ONS) or enteral feeding via nasogastric (NG) tube or gastrostomy should be considered, particularly in cases of persistent aspiration risk or inadequate intake. Follow-up should monitor respiratory symptoms during feeds, meal durations, and weight velocity to guide further interventions. This structured approach ensures safety while addressing the complex feeding and nutritional needs of children with CP and dysphagia.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CLINICAL VIGNETTE DIFFERENTIATING PICKY EATING FROM PEDIATRIC FEEDING DISORDER
subtopics.concept: Differentiating picky eating from pediatric feeding disorder (PFD) requires a structured approach to dietary assessment and feeding skills evaluation. A clinical vignette involving a 4-year-old child presenting with picky eating, characterized by refusal of many foods, insistence on repetitive meals, high mealtime conflict, and reliance on distraction feeding, highlights the importance of triaging based on growth trajectory and functional impact. Typical neophobia, a common developmental phase, must be distinguished from PFD, which involves more significant concerns. A brief diet quality screen should assess food group variety, while a targeted history should identify red flags such as weight loss, crossing percentiles, iron deficiency anemia, refusal of entire texture classes, choking, recurrent respiratory symptoms, prolonged meals with fatigue, or dependence on supplements. Observing a short meal can provide critical insights into coercion, pacing, self-feeding opportunities, and sensory responses like gagging, spitting, or pocketing. If growth is normal and no red flags are present, the condition can be managed as developmentally typical picky eating. Recommended strategies include establishing responsive feeding routines with planned meals and snacks, avoiding grazing, and offering repeated neutral exposure to new foods without pressure. Excessive milk or juice intake should be limited to prevent appetite suppression, and a measurable goal, such as one new food exposure daily for two weeks, should be set. However, if red flags are identified or family functioning is severely impacted, the child should be evaluated for suspected PFD. This requires assessing potential pain drivers such as constipation or reflux, oral-motor delays, and dysphagia risk. Multidisciplinary care involving pediatrics, dietitians, occupational therapists, speech-language pathologists, and psychologists should be initiated when available. Documentation of the functional diagnosis is essential to ensure timely escalation of care and avoid reliance on supplementation as the sole intervention. Early identification and targeted management are crucial in optimizing feeding outcomes and minimizing long-term impacts.

case_studies.title: Differentiating Picky Eating from Pediatric Feeding Disorder in a 4-Year-Old
case_studies.vignette: A 4-year-old boy is brought to a community health clinic in India with concerns about his eating habits over the past 8 months. His parents report that he refuses most foods except plain rice, biscuits, and milk, leading to repetitive meals. Mealtimes are described as stressful, with frequent tantrums and reliance on distractions like videos to encourage eating. He takes over an hour to finish meals and often spits out or gags on foods with mixed textures. The parents deny any choking episodes but note that he avoids fruits, vegetables, and meats entirely. He has not lost weight but has remained at the 10th percentile for weight since age 2. A brief dietary recall reveals a limited intake of protein and iron-rich foods. On examination, the child appears active, with no signs of pallor or nutritional deficiencies. However, a short observed meal reveals reliance on parental spoon-feeding and refusal to self-feed. No overt signs of oral-motor difficulty are noted.
case_studies.learning_objective: Differentiate developmentally typical picky eating from pediatric feeding disorder using dietary assessment, growth trajectory, and feeding skills evaluation.

subtopics.subtopic_title: CLINICAL VIGNETTE ADOLESCENT RESTRICTIVE EATING REFERRAL DECISION MAKING
subtopics.concept: A 14-year-old adolescent presenting with weight loss, meal skipping, and increasing food restriction requires a thorough and sensitive evaluation to guide referral decisions. Caregivers may report associated behaviors such as irritability and excessive exercise. The assessment begins with a confidential adolescent interview to explore intent, body image concerns, purging behaviors, and use of weight-loss products, while clearly explaining the limits of confidentiality, particularly concerning safety. A brief screening tool like the SCOFF questionnaire should be used, alongside a focused dietary history to document daily intake, avoidance rules, and potential triggers such as social media influences, bullying, or sports-related pressures. Vital signs must be measured, and medical instability assessed, including orthostatic symptoms, bradycardia, and syncope, as these findings dictate the urgency of intervention. It is critical to differentiate typical dieting from an eating disorder by identifying nutritional compromise, functional impairment, or physiological changes resulting from restrictive eating. Management extends beyond routine nutrition counseling. If danger signs, such as rapid weight loss or medical instability, are present, urgent medical evaluation is warranted, and referral to an adolescent medicine or eating disorder team should be initiated. Clinicians should order targeted laboratory tests and an ECG as indicated, and a supervised meal plan with family involvement should be implemented. Rapid unsupervised “catch-up” eating must be avoided in medically unstable patients. Comorbid conditions, including anxiety or depression, should be addressed, and suicidality assessed. Caregivers should be counseled to reduce conflict, prioritize safety, establish structured meals, and eliminate compensatory exercise behaviors until medically cleared. Close follow-up is essential to monitor progress and ensure adherence to recommendations. The working diagnosis, such as anorexia nervosa or avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder (ARFID), should be documented using DSM-5 criteria to align the multidisciplinary team’s approach. This structured framework ensures comprehensive care and facilitates collaboration among healthcare providers.

case_studies.title: Adolescent Restrictive Eating and Referral Thresholds
case_studies.vignette: A 14-year-old girl presents to a rural outpatient clinic with complaints of weight loss over the last 3 months. Her mother reports that she has been skipping meals and increasingly avoiding foods she previously enjoyed, citing concerns about 'getting fat.' The mother also notes irritability, withdrawal from family meals, and excessive exercise, including long runs daily. The adolescent admits to avoiding carbohydrates and fats but denies purging behaviors when interviewed confidentially. She expresses fear of weight gain and mentions social media influencers promoting 'clean eating.' On examination, she appears thin and pale, with a BMI in the 5th percentile for her age and height. Vital signs reveal a resting heart rate of 52 bpm and orthostatic hypotension. She reports occasional dizziness but denies fainting. A focused dietary history reveals significant caloric restriction, with intake limited to fruits and water most days. A brief SCOFF screening identifies multiple concerning responses. The family expresses concern but is unsure how to approach her eating habits.
case_studies.learning_objective: Evaluate restrictive eating behaviors in adolescents, assess medical instability, and determine referral thresholds for specialized care.
